subject: Physics
q: Two point charges <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> each are placed at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math> apart. A third point charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>q</mi></math> is placed at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> from mid-point on the perpendicular bisector. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> at which charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>q</mi></math> will experience the maximum Coulomb's force is:
question: Two point charges <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> each are placed at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math> apart. A third point charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>q</mi></math> is placed at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> from mid-point on the perpendicular bisector. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> at which charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>q</mi></math> will experience the maximum Coulomb's force is:
solution:
The distance between q and Q is r=x2+d22=x2+d24.

The force between q and Q will be F=kqQr2.

From the given figure we can see that only vertical components of the forces will add, and the horizontal components will get cancelled out. Therefore,

Fnet=2kqQr2cosθ=2kqQr2×xr=kqQxx2+d2432

For force to be maximum, dFnetdx=0.

⇒kqQx2+d2432-x×32x2+d2412×2xx2+d24322=0

⇒x2+d24=3x2⇒2x2=d24⇒x=d22

q: A ball is thrown vertically upwards with a velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>19</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> from the top of a tower. The ball strikes the ground after <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. The height from the ground up to which the ball can rise will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mfrac><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mn>5</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi></math> is _____ (use <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>9</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: A ball is thrown vertically upwards with a velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>19</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> from the top of a tower. The ball strikes the ground after <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. The height from the ground up to which the ball can rise will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mfrac><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mn>5</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi></math> is _____ (use <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>9</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
Initial velocity of ball is u=19.6 m s-1, final velocity v=0.

Using v=u+at, here, acceleration a=-g.

Time taken in upward motion above tower is ta=ug=19.69.8=2 s

Now, time taken from top most point to ground is td=6-2=4 s.

Or, td=2hmaxg    (Using s=ut+12gt2)

⇒hmax=16×9.82=3925 m.

Hence, the value of k=392.

question: An electric bulb is rated 220 volt $-100$ watt. The power consumed by it when operated on 110 volt will be

subject: Physics
q: The counting rate observed from a radioactive source at $t=0$ was 1600 counts $\mathrm{s}^{-1}$, and $t=8 \mathrm{~s}$, it was 100 counts $\mathrm{s}^{-1}$. The counting rate observed as counts $\mathrm{s}^{-1}$ at $t=6 \mathrm{~s}$ will be
question: The counting rate observed from a radioactive source at $t=0$ was 1600 counts $\mathrm{s}^{-1}$, and $t=8 \mathrm{~s}$, it was 100 counts $\mathrm{s}^{-1}$. The counting rate observed as counts $\mathrm{s}^{-1}$ at $t=6 \mathrm{~s}$ will be
solution:
As we know,
$$
\begin{aligned}
& {\left[\frac{N}{N_0}\right]=\left[\frac{1}{2}\right]^n} \\
& n=\text { no. of half life } \\
& N \text { - no. of atoms left } \\
& N_0 \text { - initial no. of atoms } \\
& \text { By radioactive decay law, } \\
& \frac{d N}{d \mathrm{t}}=k N \\
& k-\operatorname{disin} t e g r a t i o n \text { constant } \\
& \therefore \frac{\frac{d N}{d t}}{\frac{d N_0}{d t}}=\frac{N}{N_0}
\end{aligned}
$$
From (i) and (ii) we get
$$
\frac{\frac{d N}{d t}}{\frac{d N_0}{d t}}=\left[\frac{1}{2}\right]^n
$$
or, $\left[\frac{100}{1600}\right]=\left[\frac{1}{2}\right]^n \Rightarrow\left[\frac{1}{2}\right]^4=\left[\frac{1}{2}\right]^n$
$\therefore n=4$, Therefore, in 8 seconds 4 half life had occurred in which counting rate reduces to 100 counts $\mathrm{s}^{-1}$.
$\therefore$ Half life, $\frac{T_1}{2}=2 \mathrm{sec}$
In $6 \mathrm{sec}, 3$ half life will occur
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \therefore\left[\frac{\frac{d \mathrm{~N}}{d t}}{1600}\right]=\left[\frac{1}{2}\right]^3 \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{d \mathrm{~N}}{d t}=200 \text { counts } \mathrm{s}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

question: A series LCR circuit is connected to an alternating source of emf E. The current amplitude at resonant frequency is $I_0$. If the value of resistance R becomes twice of its initial value then amplitude of current at resonance will be
solution:
Initially, $\mathrm{I}_0=\frac{\varepsilon_{\mathrm{m}}}{\mathrm{R}}$
Finally, $\mathrm{I}_0^1=\frac{\varepsilon_{\mathrm{m}}}{2 \mathrm{R}}=\frac{\mathrm{I}_0}{2}$

question:
Two identical parallel plate capacitors, of capacitance C each, have plates of area A, separated by a distance d . The space between the plates of the two capacitors, is filled with three dielectrics, of equal thickness and dielectric constants K1, K2 and K3 . The first capaciitor is filled as shown in figure I, and the second one is filled as shown in figure II.
If these two modified capacitors are charged by the same potential V , the ratio of the energy stored in the two, would be (E1 refers to capacitor I and E2 to capacitor (II) ):
solution:
Energy stored in a capacitor E=12CV2
⇒E1E2=C1C2

C1=d3 ε0k1A+d3ε0Ak2+d3ε0Ak3-1
=d3Aε0-11k1+1k2+1k3-1
=d3Aε0-1k1k2+k2k3+k3k1k1k2k3-1

C1=3Aε0dk1k2k3k1k2+k2k3+k3k1
C2=ε0A3k1d+ε0A3k2d+ε0A3k3d
∴C1C2=E1E2=9 k1k2k3k1+k2+k3k1k2+k2k3+k3k1

subject: Physics
q: The given circuit works as :
question: The given circuit works as :
solution:
Let the inputs be A and B. The circuit is composed of NOR gates.
A NOR gate with its inputs tied together acts as a NOT gate.
The output of the first NOR gate (input A) is $Y_1 = \overline{A+A} = \bar{A}$.
The output of the second NOR gate (input B) is $Y_2 = \overline{B+B} = \bar{B}$.
These outputs are fed into a third NOR gate. Its output is $Y_3 = \overline{Y_1 + Y_2} = \overline{\bar{A} + \bar{B}}$.
Using De Morgan's theorem, $Y_3 = (\overline{\bar{A}}) \cdot (\overline{\bar{B}}) = A \cdot B$.
This output $Y_3$ is the input to the fourth NOR gate, which is configured as a NOT gate.
The final output is $Y_{out} = \overline{Y_3} = \overline{A \cdot B}$.
The expression $\overline{A \cdot B}$ represents a NAND gate.

q:
The root mean square speed of smoke particles of mass 5×10-17 kg in their Brownian motion in air at NTP is approximately.

[Given k=1.38×10-23J K-1]
question:
The root mean square speed of smoke particles of mass 5×10-17 kg in their Brownian motion in air at NTP is approximately.

[Given k=1.38×10-23J K-1]
solution:
According to Kinetic theory, 

Average kinetic energy of a gas molecule =12mvrms2=32kT

Thus, vrms=3kTm

At NTP, T=293 K

Putting the values, we have

=3×1.38×10-23×2935×10-17

≈15 mm s-1

subject: Physics
q: A solid sphere of mass ' $m$ ' and radius ' $r$ ' is allowed to roll without slipping from the highest point of an inclined plane of length ' $L$ ' and makes an angle $30^{\circ}$ with the horizontal. The speed of the particle at the bottom of the plane is $v_1$. If the angle of inclination is increased to $45^{\circ}$ while keeping $L$ constant. Then the new speed of the sphere at the bottom of the plane is $v_2$. The ratio $v_1^2: v_2^2$ is
question: A solid sphere of mass ' $m$ ' and radius ' $r$ ' is allowed to roll without slipping from the highest point of an inclined plane of length ' $L$ ' and makes an angle $30^{\circ}$ with the horizontal. The speed of the particle at the bottom of the plane is $v_1$. If the angle of inclination is increased to $45^{\circ}$ while keeping $L$ constant. Then the new speed of the sphere at the bottom of the plane is $v_2$. The ratio $v_1^2: v_2^2$ is
solution: using WET $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{g}}=\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{f}}-\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{i}} \\ & \mathrm{Mg} \mathrm{~L} \sin \theta=\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{f}}-\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{i}} \end{aligned}$ K.E. in pure rolling $\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mV}_{\mathrm{cm}}^2+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{cm}} \omega^2$ $\begin{aligned} & =\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mV}^2+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2}{5} \mathrm{mR}^2 \frac{\mathrm{~V}^2}{\mathrm{R}^2} \\ & \frac{7}{10} \mathrm{mV}^2 \end{aligned}$ $\mathrm{mgL} \sin \theta=\frac{7}{10} \mathrm{mV}_{\mathrm{f}}^2-0$ $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{f}}^2 \propto \sin \theta \\ & \left(\frac{\mathrm{~V}_1}{\mathrm{~V}_2}\right)^2=\frac{\sin \theta_1}{\sin \theta_2}=\frac{\sin 30^{\circ}}{\sin 45^{\circ}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A and B are two sources generating sound waves. A listener is situated at $\mathrm{C}$. The frequency of the source at $\mathrm{A}$ is $500 \mathrm{~Hz}$. A, now, moves towards $\mathrm{C}$ with a speed $4 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. The number of beats heard at $\mathrm{C}$ is 6 . When $\mathrm{A}$ moves away from $C$ with speed $4 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$, the number of beats heard at $\mathrm{C}$ is 18 . The speed of sound is $340 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. The frequency of the source at $\mathrm{B}$ is :
question: A and B are two sources generating sound waves. A listener is situated at $\mathrm{C}$. The frequency of the source at $\mathrm{A}$ is $500 \mathrm{~Hz}$. A, now, moves towards $\mathrm{C}$ with a speed $4 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. The number of beats heard at $\mathrm{C}$ is 6 . When $\mathrm{A}$ moves away from $C$ with speed $4 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$, the number of beats heard at $\mathrm{C}$ is 18 . The speed of sound is $340 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. The frequency of the source at $\mathrm{B}$ is :
solution:
$\mathrm{f}=500 \mathrm{~Hz}$
Case 1: When source is moving towards stationary listener apparent frequency $\eta^{\prime}=\eta\left(\frac{\mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{v}-\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{s}}}\right)$ $$ =500\left(\frac{340}{336}\right)=506 \mathrm{~Hz} $$ Case 2 : When source is moving away from the stationary listener $$ \eta^{\prime \prime}=\eta\left(\frac{\mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{v}+\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{s}}}\right)=500\left(\frac{340}{344}\right)=494 \mathrm{~Hz} $$ In case 1 number of beats heard is 6 and in case 2 number of beats heard is 18 therefore frequency of the source at $\mathrm{B}=512 \mathrm{~Hz}$

subject: Physics
q: A charge $Q$ is uniformly distributed over the surface of non conducting disc of radius $R$. The disc rotates about an axis perpendicular to its plane and passing through its centre with an angular velocity $\omega$. As a result of this rotation a magnetic field of induction $B$ is obtained at the centre of the disc. If we keep both the amount of charge placed on the disc and its angular velocity to be constant and vary the radius of the disc then the variation of the magnetic induction at the centre of the disc will be represented by the figure
question: A charge $Q$ is uniformly distributed over the surface of non conducting disc of radius $R$. The disc rotates about an axis perpendicular to its plane and passing through its centre with an angular velocity $\omega$. As a result of this rotation a magnetic field of induction $B$ is obtained at the centre of the disc. If we keep both the amount of charge placed on the disc and its angular velocity to be constant and vary the radius of the disc then the variation of the magnetic induction at the centre of the disc will be represented by the figure
solution: Consider ring like element of disc of radius $r$ and thickness $\mathrm{dr}$. If $\sigma$ is charge per unit area, then charge on the element $d q=\sigma(2 \pi r d r)$ current ' $i$ ' associated with rotating charge $\mathrm{dq}$ is $\mathrm{i}=\frac{(\mathrm{dq}) \mathrm{w}}{2 \pi}=\sigma \mathrm{wrdr}$ Magnetic field $\mathrm{dB}$ at center due to element $\mathrm{dB}=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{i}}{2 \mathrm{r}}=\frac{\mu_0 \sigma \omega \mathrm{dr}}{2}$ $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{B}_{\text {net }}=\int \mathrm{dB}=\frac{\mu_0 \sigma \omega}{2} \int_0^{\mathrm{R}} \mathrm{dr}=\frac{\mu_0 \sigma \omega \mathrm{R}}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{B}_{\text {net }}=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{Q} \omega}{2 \pi \mathrm{R}} \quad\left[\because \mathrm{Q}=\sigma \pi \mathrm{R}^2\right] \end{aligned}$ So if $Q$ and $w$ are unchanged then $\mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{net}} \propto \frac{1}{\mathrm{R}}$ Hence variation of $B_{\text {net }}$ with $R$ should be a rectangular hyperbola as represented in (A).

subject: Physics
q: By what percentage will the transmission range of a TV tower be affected when the height of the tower is increased by 21%?
question: By what percentage will the transmission range of a TV tower be affected when the height of the tower is increased by 21%?
solution:
The range of a TV tower antenna is given by 

d=2hR

New height is given as

h'=1.21h 

Let the new range be

 d'=2h'R⇒d'=1.212hR=1.1d

 So, the increase in the range is =1.1d-dd×100=10%

q: A ball is thrown upward with an initial velocity <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></math> from the surface of the earth. The motion of the ball is affected by a drag force equal to <math><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">γ</mi><msup><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> (where <math><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> is mass of the ball, <math><mi>v</mi></math> is its instantaneous velocity and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">γ</mi></math> is a constant). Time taken by the ball to rise to its zenith is:
question: A ball is thrown upward with an initial velocity <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></math> from the surface of the earth. The motion of the ball is affected by a drag force equal to <math><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">γ</mi><msup><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> (where <math><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> is mass of the ball, <math><mi>v</mi></math> is its instantaneous velocity and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">γ</mi></math> is a constant). Time taken by the ball to rise to its zenith is:
solution:
Net force on ball,
F=-mg-mγv2
a=-g-γv2
dvdt=-g-γv2
∫v=V0v=0dvg+γv2=∫t=0t-dt
1gγtan-1γgvv=V0v=0=-t
∴t=1γgtan-1 γgV0

subject: Physics
q: A car moving with a speed of $54$ km/h takes a turn of radius $20$ m. A simple pendulum is suspended from the ceiling of the car. Determine the angle made by the string of the pendulum with the vertical during the turning. (Take $g=10$ m/s$^2$)
question: A car moving with a speed of $54$ km/h takes a turn of radius $20$ m. A simple pendulum is suspended from the ceiling of the car. Determine the angle made by the string of the pendulum with the vertical during the turning. (Take $g=10$ m/s$^2$)
solution:
Speed of the car, $v = 54 \times \dfrac{5}{18} = 15$ m/s.

In the frame of the car, the pendulum bob experiences a pseudo force (centrifugal force) horizontally outwards and the gravitational force downwards.

Let $T$ be the tension in the string and $\theta$ be the angle it makes with the vertical. For equilibrium in the car's frame, the horizontal and vertical forces balance:

$T \sin \theta = \dfrac{mv^2}{r}$

$T \cos \theta = mg$

Dividing the two equations, we get:

$\tan \theta = \dfrac{v^2}{rg}$

Substituting the given values $v = 15$ m/s, $r = 20$ m, and $g = 10$ m/s$^2$:

$\tan \theta = \dfrac{(15)^2}{20 \times 10} = \dfrac{225}{200} = 1.125$

$\theta = \tan^{-1}(1.125)$

Answer: $\tan^{-1}(1.125)$

subject: Physics
q: A student in the laboratory measures thickness of a wire using screw gauge. The readings are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>22</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>23</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>19</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>. The percentage error is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>121</mn></mfrac><mo>%</mo></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____ .
question: A student in the laboratory measures thickness of a wire using screw gauge. The readings are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>22</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>23</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>19</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>. The percentage error is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>121</mn></mfrac><mo>%</mo></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____ .
solution:
The average value of the readings is xavg=1.19+1.20+1.22+1.234=1.21

Now, absolute error in measurement Δxavg=0.02+0.01+0.01+0.024=0.064=0.015

Percentage error in measurement is =0.0151.21×100%=150121%

So, the value of x=150.

subject: Physics
q: A block moving horizontally on a smooth surface with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> splits into two equal parts. If one of the parts moves at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> in the same direction, then the fractional change in the kinetic energy will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mn>4</mn></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo></math> ________.
question: A block moving horizontally on a smooth surface with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> splits into two equal parts. If one of the parts moves at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> in the same direction, then the fractional change in the kinetic energy will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mn>4</mn></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo></math> ________.
solution:
2m×40=60×m+mv
V=20 m/s
Now ΔKE=KEf-KEi=12 m×(20)2+12 m×(60)2-122 m×(40)2
ΔKE=12 m(800)⇒KEi=122 m×(40)2=12 m×3200
Fractional change =ΔKEKEi=14

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements : one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R)
Assertion (A) : The property of body, by virtue of which it tends to regain its original shape when the external force is removed, is Elasticity.
Reason (R) : The restoring force depends upon the bonded inter atomic and inter molecular force of solid.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements : one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R)
Assertion (A) : The property of body, by virtue of which it tends to regain its original shape when the external force is removed, is Elasticity.
Reason (R) : The restoring force depends upon the bonded inter atomic and inter molecular force of solid.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: Both (A) and (R) are true and (R) is the correct explanation (A)
Both (A) and (R) are true but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A)
solution:
Elasticity is the ability of a material to deform under stress and then return to its original shape when the stress is removed.

The restoring force in elastic materials is associated with the inter-atomic or inter-molecular forces that act to bring the atoms or molecules back to their original positions when the material is deformed. When an external force is applied to a material, it causes a displacement of atoms or molecules from their equilibrium positions. The restoring force is a result of the potential energy stored in these inter-atomic or inter-molecular bonds, and it works to bring the material back to its original state.

Hence, both (A) and (R) are true and (R) is the correct explanation (A)

subject: Physics
q:
The helium and argon are put in the flask at the same room temperature ( 300 K). The ratio of average kinetic energies (per molecule) of helium and argon is :
(Give : Molar mass of helium $=4 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}$, Molar mass of argon $=40 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}$)
question:
The helium and argon are put in the flask at the same room temperature ( 300 K). The ratio of average kinetic energies (per molecule) of helium and argon is :
(Give : Molar mass of helium $=4 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}$, Molar mass of argon $=40 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}$)
solution:
$K. E=\frac{f}{2} K T$
For He and $\mathrm{Ar} \mathrm{f}=3$
$\frac{\mathrm{K} \cdot \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{He}}}{\mathrm{~K} \cdot \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Ar}}}=\frac{1}{1}$

subject: Physics
q:
In the following nuclear reaction,
D→αD1→β-D2→αD3→γD4
Mass number of D is 182 and atomic number is 74 . Mass number and atomic number of D4 respectively will be
question:
In the following nuclear reaction,
D→αD1→β-D2→αD3→γD4
Mass number of D is 182 and atomic number is 74 . Mass number and atomic number of D4 respectively will be
solution:
Equivalent reaction can be written as D→D4+2α+β-+γ

So, mass number of D4=mass number of D-2×4=182-8=174

Atomic number of D4=atomic number of D-2×2+1=74-4+1=71

The reaction is 

D74182⟶αD172178⟶β-D273178⟶αD371174→γD471174

subject: Physics
q: A person pushes a box on a rough horizontal plateform surface. He applies a force of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi>N</mi></math> over a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi>m</mi></math>. Thereafter, he gets progressively tired and his applied force reduces linearly with distance to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>N</mi></math>. The total distance through which the box has been moved is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi>m</mi></math>. What is the work done by the person during the total movement of the box?
question: A person pushes a box on a rough horizontal plateform surface. He applies a force of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi>N</mi></math> over a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi>m</mi></math>. Thereafter, he gets progressively tired and his applied force reduces linearly with distance to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>N</mi></math>. The total distance through which the box has been moved is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi>m</mi></math>. What is the work done by the person during the total movement of the box?
solution:
W=area=(200×15)+12(100+200)×15

=3000+2250

W=5250 J
